---
title: ""
subtitle: ""
date: YYYY-MM-DD
categories: ["Methods and Models"]
image: hero.png
image-alt: ""
description: ""
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
bibliography: references.bib
draft: true

# Private social copy (not rendered). Copy to platform on publish.
social-x: |
  X / Twitter version (280 chars max).

social-li: |
  LinkedIn version (~1500 chars).
---

![](hero.png){.preview-image fig-align="center"}

Lead with the claim. State what the post will argue and why it matters. Two to four sentences.

# Introduction

Frame the problem. What is the current state of thinking? What is missing? What will this post add?

# Main argument

Develop the case. Cite evidence. Show code if it sharpens the argument. Use figures where they replace a paragraph of description.

```{r}
#| label: example-chunk
#| echo: true
#| fig-cap: "Example figure caption"

# code here
```

# Counter-arguments

Acknowledge the strongest counter-claims. Address them or concede where warranted. This is the dialectic move that separates serious posts from hot takes.

# What follows

What would need to be true for the argument to be wrong? What is the next thing to test or write?

# References
